FAMILY LAW – CHILDREN – Best interests – With whom a child lives – With whom a child spends time – Parental responsibility – Where the father has relocated interstate – Where the parties have no capacity to liaise – Where no party proposed any safe or practically realistic orders for the children’s reintroduction to their father.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

JUSTICE CARTER

INTRODUCTION

This is a difficult and complicated matter. The parties have three children – X who is ten, Y who is eight and Z who is six. There is no agreement as to what arrangements are in their best interests.
The children live with their mother in City B. They have not seen their father since November 2022. The father has now relocated to Queensland and remarried.
The mother genuinely believes each child has been sexually abused by the father and is unable to contemplate the children spending any time with him. The father strenuously denies the allegations. He seeks an immediate change of residence, or, alternatively, that the children be reintroduced to him.
The Independent Children's Lawyer supports the mother’s proposals that the children live with her and have no time with their father.
The police (Sexual Offences and Child Abuse Investigation Team (“SOCIT”) and the Department of Families Fairness and Housing conducted investigations between 2022 and 2024. No charges were laid against the father, and both agencies have closed their matters. The children have had extensive counselling through C Mental Health Services.
For the reasons that follow:
(a) I am not satisfied on the balance of probabilities that the alleged abuse occurred; and

(b) I am not satisfied that the father presents an unacceptable risk of sexual harm to the children.

However, there is no identified, realistic or safe pathway to re-introduce the children to their father at this time. The least worst outcome for the children is that they remain living with their mother, and have no time or communication with their father.

THE EVIDENCE

It has not been possible to include every aspect of each of the parties’ evidence. However, I have taken all the evidence into account. Just because I have not mentioned something in these reasons does not mean that I have not considered it.
Section 140 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth) sets out that the standard of proof in these proceedings is to a balance of probabilities. In accordance with the principles established in Briginshaw v Briginshaw [1938] HCA 34; (1938) 60 CLR 336 (‘Briginshaw v Briginshaw’) at [362] in circumstances where the allegations are of a particularly serious nature, more convincing evidence is required to establish that allegation.

The Mother

The mother relied on the following documents:
(a) the mother’s trial affidavit filed 23 March 2025;

(b) the mother’s reply affidavit filed 14 April 2025;

(c) the mother’s trial affidavit filed 22 September 2025;

(d) Amended Response to Initiating Application filed 24 March 2025;

(e) affidavit of Ms D, the mother’s psychologist, filed 21 April 2025; and

(f) affidavit of Ms E, the mother and X’s counsellor, filed 24 April 2025.

The mother, Ms D and Ms E each gave evidence and were cross examined.

The Father

The father relied on the following documents:
(a) the father’s trial affidavit filed 25 March 2025;

(b) the father’s reply affidavit filed 14 April 2025;

(c) the father’s trial affidavit filed 25 September 2025;

(d) Amended Initiating Application filed 24 March 2025;

(e) affidavit of Mr F filed 24 March 2025;

(f) affidavit of Ms G filed 24 March 2025;

(g) affidavit of Ms J filed 24 March 2025; and

(h) affidavit of Dr H, the father’s psychologist, filed 25 March 2025.

The father, Ms G, Dr H, Ms J and Mr F gave evidence and were cross examined.

Other experts

The Court also had the benefit of evidence from the following single experts:
(a) Family Report of Ms K filed 27 February 2025;

(b) affidavit of expert Dr Ben Jones, Clinical Psychologist, filed 26 June 2025;

(c) affidavit of Dr Andrea Vergara, Forensic Psychologist, filed 17 April 2025;

(d) affidavit of Dr Andrea Vergara, filed 11 July 2025; and

(e) addendum report of Dr Ben Jones filed 8 October 2025.

Each expert was required to give evidence and was cross examined.

Tendered documents

At the commencement of the trial, the parties were advised that r 8.15(3) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) applied to all documents exhibited to the affidavits of each lay witness. Pursuant to that rule, any document sought to be relied upon would not be accepted as evidence unless and until it was separately tendered. Accordingly, the lay witness affidavits read into evidence did not include any of the exhibits annexed.
A large number of additional reports and documents were tendered into evidence in the course of the hearing.

BACKGROUND

The parents commenced their relationship in 2007. They moved to City L, Queensland in 2010.
In 2012 the father said he suffered a workplace accident. The parties then moved to Town M, Victoria where the father secured a senior position in a public service role.
X was born in 2015. The mother ceased working shortly before X was born. She has remained engaged as a full-time parent since.
Y was born in 2017.
In 2018 the parties returned to live in City B, Victoria where the father was employed at N School. He later asserted he experienced workplace bullying there.
Z was born in 2019.
By 2020 the father was seeking medical assistance to help manage symptoms of depression and anxiety.
X commenced with Ms E, psychologist in January 2021 following a mental health referral. Ms E was engaged to assist with X’s separation anxiety and to support her to commence school in early 2021. The mother commenced treatment with her psychologist, Ms D.
When X commenced school, she started on a part time basis. It was the mother’s evidence that X continued to struggle to separate from her, and she worked with Ms E until about March 2022. By around mid-2021 X’s anxieties had substantially subsided, and she was attending school on a full time basis.
In August 2021 the father was assessed by Dr O, psychiatrist following what the psychiatrist described as a “near attempt recently” to take his own life. To Dr O the father described his depressive symptoms as worsening over the last couple of months, that he felt tired and exhausted, with poor motivation and sleep disturbances. He reported;
...having more suicidal thoughts, at least once a day and had a plan to gas self 2–3 weeks back; however his youngest son woke up and he couldn’t go ahead with it.
The father’s medication dosage was increased, with a recommendation that the medication be changed if the increased dose did not assist.
On about 9 July 2022, and in the following days, Z – then two years old – made several statements that caused the parents to suspect that the maternal uncle had sexually abused Z. The parents engaged C Mental Health Services to provide Z with counselling support. It is not in dispute that C Mental Health Services do not forensically investigate whether abuse has occurred. Their mandate is to work with victim survivors.
C Mental Health Services notified Child Protection. SOCIT were also notified.
The Department of Families Fairness and Housing closed their report at intake without investigation.
It was the mother’s evidence that in late August and early September 2022 Y told her the father had sexually abused Y.
Y and Z participated in visual and audio record evidence interviews (“VARE”) with City B SOCIT in late August 2022. Neither child made any disclosures.
On that day, Child Protection received a second notification.
The father moved out of the former matrimonial home on about 8 September 2022.
The father and the mother were interviewed by Child Protection in mid-September 2022. Y was also interviewed the same day by Child Protection. Again, no disclosures were made.
The father’s mental health deteriorated after he moved out of the home. In mid-September 2022 the father took a deliberate overdose of prescription medication including oxycodone, tramadol and tapentadol. The mother called the father and he told her he had taken “hundreds of pills”. He then sent the mother a text message saying what hotel he was at, and that he would not be calling an ambulance. The mother attended at the motel and called an ambulance. The father had written letters to the mother apologising for being a disappointment and saying he would never do anything to harm the children. He also wrote notes for each of the children saying he would miss them and that he loved them.
The father was admitted as an involuntary inpatient at P Health Services where he was in involuntary inpatient for five nights.
In mid-September 2022 the father spent some time at the home, at the request of the mother, he made a cake for X.
The mother was referred to Q Centre in about late September 2022.
The father was admitted voluntarily into R Recovery Centre in late September 2022, where he remained an inpatient until mid-October 2022.
The mother facilitated some short visits between the father and the children after he was discharged.
The father continued to experience poor mental health following his discharge from R Recovery Centre. He reported feeling anxious and experiencing suicidal thoughts on about 7 November 2022. He experienced several panic attacks over the course of a few days. He was due to return to work in mid-November 2022. He did not do so.
On 16 November 2022 the father travelled to City S in New South Wales to visit his grandmother. The father has remained living interstate since that time.
In December 2022 the mother obtained a Family Violence Intervention Order listing herself and the children as protected persons.
In January 2023 Y commenced counselling with C Mental Health Services. Initially he attended there twice weekly, and then on a weekly basis until about June 2024. X also attended a session in early 2023.
The mother re-engaged Ms E to assist with X and herself in early 2023. The mother said X was experiencing anxiety around school and bedtime and struggled to sleep in her bedroom. Ms E asserted she confined her counselling for X to concerns other than sexual abuse. She said X’s concerns were ongoing peer difficulties, anxieties regarding school teachers, X’s participation in gymnastics and fears associated with sleeping in her own bed. Her evidence was that X’s reluctance to sleep in her bedroom could be suggestive of abuse. Ms E confirmed X has never made a disclosure to her. Based on the mother’s comments to her regarding the children’s statements and on her concerns for X, Ms E expressed a view these matters were suggestive of “links to sexual abuse”.
X commenced counselling at C Mental Health Services on about a weekly basis in June 2023.
Z commenced appointments with C Mental Health Services from about July 2023, again attended generally weekly or fortnightly until June 2024.
Y and Z both made a statement consistent with possible sexual abuse to C Mental Health Services for the first time in about mid-2023.
X made a statement consistent with possible sexual abuse to C Mental Health Services for the first time in September 2023.
Z participated in a second VARE in mid-September 2023. Z made statements about “honey poo” coming out of the father’s penis and bottom.
X participated in a VARE in late November 2023. X made statements that the father had touched her and Z on the bottom and put his finger in their bottoms.
In 2024 X’s appointments with C Mental Health Services reduced to once every two to three weeks.
The Department of Families Fairness and Housing conducted further interviews with X and Y in late May 2024.
The children ‘took a break’ from counselling with C Mental Health Services in about mid-2024.
The parents were interviewed by Dr Jones on 3 June 2024. Dr Jones prepared psychological assessments of each of them.
In mid-2024 the father filed a proposed Statement of Claim in the County Court of Victoria in which he sought damages as a result a what he described as a “permanent severe psychiatric condition, arising out of my employment at [N School]”.
Z was interviewed by Department of Families Fairness and Housing again in early July 2024.
SOCIT closed their case in mid-August 2024. The Department of Families Fairness and Housing closed their investigation a few days later in August 2024. The father was not charged with any offences.
In August 2024 the mother and children vacated the former matrimonial home and moved in with the maternal grandparents. In early 2025 the mother secured rental accommodation in City B for herself and the children.
The parents attended on the Family Report writer for interviews on 22 January 2025.
The father attended upon Dr Vergara on 20 March 2025 for the purposes of the preparation of a psychosexual assessment.
The mother filed her trial affidavit on 23 March 2025 and the father on 25 March 2025.
The father filed further affidavits in support of his personal injury application before the County Court in mid-2025.
The matter was initially listed for trial before me on 28 April 2025. However, at the first day of the hearing I was advised that no party had subpoenaed the VARE recordings or the SOCIT records. Additionally, a complete and unredacted set of notes from C Mental Health Services had not yet been provided. Counsel all agreed the matter could not proceed in the absence of this evidence and the final hearing was adjourned.
A two-day hearing took place in the County Court in mid-2025 to establish whether the father had a serious injury. The father was cross examined in that court. The decision was reserved. If that court determines he has suffered a serious injury, the father intends to pursue a common law claim for damages. I have not been advised as to the outcome, if any, of those proceedings.
The hearing before me commenced on 29 September 2025. It ran for ten days and concluded on 31 October 2025.

THE CASES ADVANCED

When opening his case, the father sought orders for an immediate change of residence and that he have sole decision-making responsibility for the three children. He sought a positive finding that the abuse did not occur. It was his case that the only outcome in which the children could come to understand they were not abused, was if there was a change of residence.
For the reasons set out in this judgment, that proposal appeared unworkable.
In the event the Court determined the children would remain living with the mother, the alternate position proposed by the father was that there be a scaffolded re-introduction for the children to spend time with him. There were also significant practical difficulties with that proposal.
At closing, counsel for the father submitted that if the Court was to make orders pursuant to which the children were re-introduced to the father, those orders should be made on an interim basis. Also in closing, counsel asserted the first step did not necessarily need to be a therapeutically supported reintroduction, but the children should be observed with their father, so that there could be a “testing” of whether the children recognised him and their responses to him could be observed. Counsel for the father said that would give an indication as to whether the children were fearful of their father and if therapy was even needed.
The Independent Children's Lawyer and the mother proposed the children live with the mother and she have sole decision-making responsibility for them. The Independent Children's Lawyer supported the mother’s proposal there be no time or communication between the children and their father.
The mother also sought a number of orders, including orders permitting her to change the children’s names, obtain passports for the children and a number of restraints.
Given the manner in which the allegations unfolded, counsel for the mother conceded the evidence did not permit a positive finding that abuse occurred. However, the mother’s case was that the father nevertheless presents an unacceptable risk of harm to the children. The risks identified were the sexual safety of the children, as well as risks of harm to the children’s emotional and psychological safety for reasons including the father’s fixed views of the mother and his history of mental ill health. In addition, it was asserted that no orders could be safely made for the father’s re-introduction to the children as the impact on the mother of any such order would have a deleterious effect on her parenting capacity. Moreover, there was no articulation of how a therapeutically supported reintroduction could practically be implemented given that the father lives interstate.

THE CHILDREN’S STATEMENTS

I have set out substantially chronologically the matters the mother outlined in her material, and as taken from documents tendered and evidence given in these proceedings. A careful consideration of the evidence demonstrates that the children’s statements escalated over time. Many were elicited at bedtime, often after prompting or leading questions by the mother, and in circumstances where the children received rewards of their mother’s attention and a delaying of going to bed.
The statements made to persons other than the mother were only after prolonged involvement of C Mental Health Services, utilising literature about “bad touching” and “secrets” with affirmation of disclosures. There were fantastical elements to some statements, inconsistencies between siblings and some equivocation, particularly by X, about what was seen, felt or remembered.
On 9 July 2022 Z told the mother that someone had touched his penis. When asked by the mother “who”, Z said it was the maternal uncle. When asked “how” Z said he had been touched with a “pear”. The following day when the mother asked Z if his uncle changed his nappy, Z said his uncle had hurt his bottom with a pencil. Later that day the mother raised with Z whether he had said the word “pear” or “pat” the day before, and Z then referred to his penis and said “[the uncle’s] penis”. The mother asked if his uncle had a penis and what it looked like. Z said it was painted orange and purple and blue, and it had poo on it.
The parents decided to seek support from C Mental Health Services. They reported the allegations to SOCIT and Child Protection.
C Mental Health Services conducted an intake assessment in mid-July 2022. There were seven sessions conducted with the mother between July 2022 and August 2022, via telephone. At that time the mother commenced making handwritten notes of things Z said or did and reported them to C Mental Health Services.
The mother made a note of a conversation she had with Z on 14 July 2022. She said Z said his uncle put his finger in his bottom, and put a pear in his bottom, and that it was orange, and that he put fruit in his bottom.
On 23 July 2022 the mother made a note that Z said Santa was coming and “he going to eat my penis”. The mother asked Z if something like that had ever happened, and Z said yes, his uncle had touched his penis.
Later that day Z told his mother his bottom was still not better and that his uncle had hurt it.
On 31 July 2022 the mother made a note that Z put his hands in his nappy and said he made a knot in his penis and that his penis had made soup. In response to a question by the mother if he had seen a penis make soup, Z said his uncle made soup “and it go on my penis”.
On 3 August 2022 at bedtime the mother made another note that Z said his penis “won’t work” and that he was scared someone would hurt him. His mother asked if someone hurt him and he said his uncle.
On 7 August 2022 the father made a video of the boys playing together which he sent to the mother. Y can just be heard whispering about putting something in their bottoms and babies. They were aged five and two.
On 9 August 2022 the mother made a note at 8.20 pm that Z said he had needed his mother “to stop” his uncle “jumping on me”. Z then said his uncle was “eating my pants” and that Z ended up in his uncle’s tummy.
On 20 August 2022 the mother made a note that when she took a photograph of a rash on Z’s stomach she asked if anyone else had taken photos of Z’s tummy. He said his uncle did. When asked what happened, he responded, “bouncing up and down”. He then spoke about a “robot” and “flying and bouncing”.
The mother evidently was troubled by these comments. Her evidence was that she believes these statements by Z suggested he was being sexually abused. The mother does not suggest that Z was ever upset or distressed when making these statements.
At trial the mother acknowledged it was unlikely that the uncle would have had any opportunity to abuse Z as they had only seen the maternal uncle at family gatherings as Christmas and Easter.
SOCIT determined to take no action. This frustrated the father and distressed the mother. Similarly, the Department of Families Fairness and Housing closed their investigation, having assessed the parents as protective.
At trial the mother said the father had been driving the process with the authorities and had wanted Z to be interviewed. It is difficult to understand why the father would have been supportive of the involvement of the SOCIT and Child Protection if he had been sexually abusing the children.
The mother acknowledged that in August 2022 she read a book to the children called “Everyone’s Got a Bottom”. That is a book designed to teach children about body parts, private body parts and rules about body safety.
The mother deposed in about August 2022, Y asked her “what would happen if something happened a long time ago”. She was advised by C Mental Health Services to ask Y open questions.
The mother further deposed that on 30 August 2022 Y told her that the father had asked him if Y wanted to touch his “private parts”. She said the father was at work when that was said. The mother said she called the father and said the police might want to speak with Y. She said the father then came home from work early, extremely anxious, agitated and on edge. She said that when the father put Y to bed that night, she heard the father say to Y “don’t tell anyone”. She said she heard this through the baby monitor. She assumed this was the father telling Y not to disclose the father’s abuse of him. She said she panicked and her “protective instincts kicked in”. Her evidence was Y subsequently said he had told the father that he had “told mummy” about their secret.
Y and Z were interviewed by SOCIT in late August 2022 and participated in VARE interviews. Neither child made any disclosures.
At his interview, Z was asked about his bottom being hurt and he said he had hurt it himself and demonstrated falling down, and his mother had put cream on it. When asked about “the pencil” he said he had fallen over, as he had been knocked by another boy and the boy said sorry. He said he fell over at school.
When asked if he had seen someone’s penis, Z said yes, he saw his own. When asked who else’s penis he saw he said “nobody”. When asked what happened when his uncle changed his nappy Z said “I don’t know”. He was asked several times “what did [uncle] do” and he was not responsive. He was asked again “did [uncle] hurt your bottom” and he said “No. I hurt my bum all by myself”. He was asked if anything “yucky” happened when [uncle] changed his nappy. He said “poo”. Z was again asked “what happened when [uncle] changed your nappy?” which was not responded to. The interview concludes:
Officer: I asked you before about did you see anyone’s penis?

[Z]: No.

Officer: No? Did you touch someone’s penis?

[Z]: No.

Officer: No? Did someone touch your penis?

[Z]: No.
The mother told C Mental Health Services that Z had felt intimidated by SOCIT staff who had been stern with him. Having viewed the VARE, I do not agree that anyone interacting with Z appeared to be stern.
At his interview, Y frequently responded “don’t know” to many questions. He could not identify anything that the father did that was fun, or not so fun. When told that his mother had told them something about “what Dad did”, Y responded “I don’t remember” on multiple occasions. He got very upset towards the end of the interview.
Notwithstanding that Z denied anyone touched his penis, the mother persisted in asking Z about possible abuse. For instance, at her interview with the Department of Families Fairness and Housing in mid-September 2022 the mother reported that she asked Z if the person [who had touched his penis] was a man or a lady and he said “Daddy”.
Y was interviewed by the Department of Families Fairness and Housing in mid-September 2022. He made no disclosures. He answered many questions with “I don’t know” and when asked if he bathed himself, he replied “secrets”.
By this time, it appears the mother was interpreting a number of the children’s statements and behaviours as an indication that they had been subjected to sexual abuse by their father. She continued to frequently report many statements and behaviours to C Mental Health Services. It appears the statements and behaviours were not examined critically by the mother or by those at C Mental Health Services, but accepted as ‘evidence’ that the children had been abused. This was even when the statements or behaviours were unclear or ambiguous, and could have had an alternate explanation, such as the children being silly, engaging in exploratory, or self‑soothing behaviours.
In mid-September 2022 the mother reported to C Mental Health Services that Z’s demeanour after the SOCIT interviews had changed and he was angrier. She also referred to him saying “it is fly time” and then sucking his fingers before saying “I want daddy” and then he hit the mother in the face. He subsequently said, “[Uncle] ate my tummy”.
A C Mental Health Services note of that date records the mother as “praising [Z] and [X]” for “speaking up”. At some point Y apparently told his mother he was “joking” about what he said regarding “private parts”. The mother reported Y then told her that when he said he was joking, he was “lying”. The note further records that Y spoke with the mother the night before about “private parts” and said he was “not sure if it was a dream or real”, and that maybe it was a dream “because adults are not supposed to do that”.
In early October 2022 the mother reported to C Mental Health Services that Y told the mother he and the father watched YouTube clips of boats and ships together in Y’s bed. The mother said this was striking as she had not known the father to get into Y’s bed. Y then became distressed and was sobbing and said “I just want daddy”. The note by C Mental Health Services recorded that they provided the mother with “psycho education re trauma responses in children”. Neither the mother nor the counsellor appeared to consider Y’s distress could be because he was missing his father.
The following day in early October 2022 the mother reported to C Mental Health Services that Y was touching his genital area frequently and said he was missing his father whilst he had his hands in his pants. The mother also reported that at bedtime Y put his bottom in the air whilst on his hands and feet. The mother connected this to the father being the person who usually put the child to bed and that the father had made “significant attempts” to ensure it was he who put Y to bed. It appears the mother interpreted these behaviours as reinforcing her concerns that Y had been sexually abused by his father, rather than indicating the child’s distress could be a result of the father no longer living in the home.
The mother deposed that on 5 November 2022 Z said “someone put poo on me... in my bottom and my penis” and “monster put spanner in my penis and my bottom” and “he eat me”. The mother made a note of this.
The mother reported to C Mental Health Services in early November 2022 that Y asked how he can “find out if something is a dream or not” and that it was “about the private parts”. The worker said they discussed with the mother about “encouraging open discussion without possible leading questions”.
The mother made another note on 12 November 2022 that Z said a monster had put poo in his bottom at nighttime.
The children last saw their father on about 16 November 2022 at a park. In her evidence the mother acknowledged she was nervous for the children’s safety and conceded the children would have picked up on her anxiety about them being physically near their father.
In late November 2022 the mother told C Mental Health Services that Y had said he used to watch videos with his father at nighttime of “planes crashing into bridges” and that his father woke him up in the night to do this. The mother said Y said his tummy was sore and had asked multiple questions about children going to jail and “keeping a secret”. The mother also reported that X said the father gave her a “full body” massage and then X “later put her hands in her pants”.
Two days later in November 2022 the mother reported to C Mental Health Services that Y told her the father had asked him if he wanted to touch his bottom, when he had no clothes on, in the ensuite bathroom. According to the C Mental Health Services note the mother suggested “he might want to draw instead of speak”. The mother acknowledged that was her suggestion to Y.
The following evening Y asked to draw pictures at bedtime. The mother said Y drew a picture and said it was X showing her vagina to Y, the father and Z.
The mother said Y drew another picture and said it was the father in the bathroom asking Y if he wanted to touch his bottom. He said it had ‘happened again’. Y also drew a picture of a stage. The mother linked this to X’s dance concert the year before, and she said Y said “something happened” but provided no detail. The mother also reported to C Mental Health Services that she now felt “uneasy” about the times she left the children alone with their father in the past, and “holds doubts about [the father’s] intentions when he would push for her to leave the house”.
In early December 2022 the mother reported to C Mental Health Services that “each night [Y] requests” to draw and have the mother write down what he said. The mother said the pictures included a drawing of the children in a room with their father, with everyone showing their bottoms, and then having ice cream; and a drawing of Y’s bottom and his father with a penis. The mother reported Y said he felt “something itchy on my leg and bottom sometime...more than one time”.
The following day in December 2022 the mother told C Mental Health Services that Y had made a “very detailed” drawing the evening before of the father sexually abusing him. The mother told C Mental Health Services that she had “no doubt in her mind” that Y had been sexually assaulted. At trial she was unable to identify the “very detailed” drawing she had provided to C Mental Health Services at that time.
Three days later in early December 2022 the mother told C Mental Health Services that Y “continues to disclose via drawing most nights at bed time” – described as a “ritual of disclosing most nights prior to bed”. She said the drawings included the father with no pants on. She said Y said the father touched Y’s penis with his penis and told Y not to tell the mother. She said Y initially asked her to guess the identity of the people he had drawn. She said after he has drawn he becomes very quiet.
On that day, the mother also told C Mental Health Services that Z had bounced up and down on her pelvic area. She asked if Z had done that before and he said yes. The mother asked if he had pants on and Z said no. He said the children and the father were “running around... everyone had their pants off... Daddy had his tongue out and was making a silly sound”.
In mid-December 2022 the mother told C Mental Health Services the nightly drawings with Y continued. She told them Y drew a picture that “looked different to previous” and Y said it was “at Coles... the toilets” but he did not say anything further.
The mother also reported that many of Y’s drawings were just of the three children. The mother said she thought the children may have continued to engage in sexualised behaviour with each other without the father. She said she had not witnessed this at that stage but had “a gut feeling”.
Y commenced counselling with C Mental Health Services in about late 2022. He continued until March 2024. The intake records said, “most of [Y’s] disclosures about his father have been provided by [Y] when he is being put to bed by [the mother]”. The mother reported that Y usually draws and then asked the mother to describe what he has drawn. The records of Y’s appointments in early 2023 set out the counsellor discussed with how he feels when he is scared or does not feel safe, to identify “early warning signs” and the importance of talking to an adult about bad or worrying things.
X and Y attended a counsellor at C Mental Health Services in early January 2023 with the mother. The counsellor talked about grown-ups being supposed to keep children safe. The children were asked what they were worried about. X responded that she was worried about cartwheels on the beam at gymnastics. Y said he did not know of any worries he had. He attended again on two further occasions in January. He again made no statements consistent with the sexual abuse allegations and repeated that he did not have any worries.
The mother deposed that in early 2023 X said “words to the effect” that the father had “touched her in an inappropriate manner”. X also said she played a game with her brothers and father, similar to hide and seek but naked.
In early February 2023 a report was prepared by C Mental Health Services with respect to Y. He had not made any disclosures to workers there despite having attended for ten appointments between December 2022 and February 2023 – including seven appointments in January. Notwithstanding, the assessment included a statement that the father “appears to have successfully groomed the children” and that the father “will manipulate and hide the truth in order to have his sexual needs gratified”. These conclusions were based only on the mother’s reports as by that time the children had not made statements potentially consistent with sexual abuse to anyone else. The assessment also noted that “most of [Y’s] disclosures about his father have been provided by [Y] when he is being put to bed by [the mother]”.
The mother made a note that on 4 February 2023 the children were being silly at bathtime with a toy camera. When the mother asked if the children had been silly and naked before she wrote that X “looked uncomfortable” and said yes, that Y and Z had no clothes on, and “daddy was being silly looking for his pants. He had none on”.
The mother deposed that on 7 February 2023 X told her that the father gave her a massage “everywhere on her body”.

THE FAMILY REPORT WRITER

The report dated 25 February 2025 was written prior to an interim hearing. The writer concluded that any reintroduction to the father would need to occur “within in the context of a therapeutic and supported environment”. According to the notes provided by Dr H, the father told him on 30 January 2025 that the Family Report writer said he was “likely to get custody as kids [were] currently not doing well” and there was “no evidence against him”. It is difficult to understand how the father gained that impression.
At trial, the Family Report writer accepted that every option carried significant risks. If the children remained with the mother and the allegations of abuse were unfounded, their wellbeing would be at risk if a false narrative were maintained. If they were removed from the mother and placed with the father, the move would likely be highly disruptive, confusing, and distressing. Any reintroduction to their father would need to be very carefully managed by specialists experienced in reunification work, given the significant complexities affecting these children and their parents.
Counsel for the father criticised the Family Report on several bases including: the absence of observations between the father and the children; the brevity of the children’s interviews; and alleged over-reliance on Child Protection material said to be flawed. Counsel also suggested the writer’s reluctance to support a change of residence was based on an assumption that the children would be fearful of their father. It was submitted that an observation session may have illuminated whether the children were in fact fearful.
I did not form the impression that the Family Report writer placed undue weight on the material from the Department of Families Fairness and Housing or C Mental Health Services. I do not accept the submission that she demonstrated an “unconscious bias” as a result of relying on those reports. They formed part of the information available to her and were appropriately considered.
Counsel for the father also criticised the Family Report writer for referring to C Mental Health Services as a “specialist service”. I do not accept that criticism. C Mental Health Services is a specialist service providing support and treatment for survivors of sexual assault.
The father’s counsel further contended that the absence of observations between the father and the children was procedurally unfair. There is, however, no requirement that a Family Report include such observations. The decision not to conduct observation sessions was reasonable given the children had not seen their father for a protracted period and serious allegations of sexual abuse had been made. I do not agree that, without an observation session, the Court cannot be satisfied that the children would likely be fearful of their father. The Family Report writer is well placed to give an opinion on the children’s likely reactions.
Similarly, the decision not to interview Z did not undermine the report. The children had already been interviewed repeatedly by SOCIT, Child Protection, and in multiple C Mental Health Services sessions. The Family Report writer was properly mindful of this history.
I also reject the submission that the writer should not have accepted that the children believed they had been sexually abused by their father and were likely fearful of him, or that these findings improperly influenced her views concerning a change of residence. It is, of course, possible that the children may not be fearful. However, given their experiences, the extensive counselling, and the mother’s expressed views, I accept that they likely believe their father abused them. It is therefore far more probable that reintroduction to their father, or placement in his care, would be deeply confusing and confronting.
Even leaving aside the allegations of abuse, the Family Report writer held concerns about the distress and dislocation the children would experience if they were removed from their mother’s care, their community, and their schools in City B and relocated to Queensland to live with the father and his new wife.
The writer maintained that any reintroduction to the father would require careful planning with practitioners experienced in complex family law matters. The children would require therapeutic support to manage any fears or confusion. I note Ms E’s evidence that she would not be the appropriate counsellor if X needed therapy to understand she had not been abused; her answer suggested she held the view that X had been abused. X would therefore require a new psychologist in any reunification process.
The Family Report writer also said the mother would require therapy to manage her own fears and anxieties. Additionally, the father’s time with the children would need initially to be supervised. Any progression would depend on how the reintroduction unfolded, and interim orders would likely be required.
The Family Report writer was not aware of any suitably qualified professionals in the City B area. She suggested possible assistance could be sought from W Centre, AA Hospital or private practitioners such as Ms BB or Ms CC, all located in Melbourne. The children might be able to utilise referrals from the Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service for private practitioners to support them. She emphasised the process would not be quick and would be challenging for both parents and the children. Additionally, the children might reject any therapist who challenged their views about their father.
I found the Family Report writer’s evidence to be considered and thoughtful. Her observations were reasonable and well founded. A testing of her evidence did not reveal any bias.

CREDIT FINDINGS

In parenting matters, general credibility findings are rarely necessary, as inconsistencies often arise from differing perceptions rather than dishonesty. In this case, however, the father’s evidence must be treated with caution. While some inaccuracies may stem from emotionally charged reconstruction, several matters raise serious concerns regarding his reliability.
First, the father presented incompatible accounts of his mental health to this Court, to Dr Jones, and in separate, contemporaneous County Court proceedings. In June 2024 the father told Dr Jones he was emotionally stable, free of suicidal ideation, the happiest he had ever been and not taking medication. Yet later, he filed a County Court claim asserting he suffered a permanent severe mental or behavioural disorder, supported by an affidavit describing extensive symptoms including anxiety, avoidance of social settings, sleep disturbance, impaired concentration, suicidal thoughts, loss of motivation and the use of Venlafaxine. He also deposed that he could not go near a school, that he struggled when he heard a bell or drove in a school zone, and that school uniforms were a “trigger” for him.
The father accepted he did not advise Dr Jones he was contemplating making this application and did not advise the mother or this Court of the County Court proceedings until referring to them in his Trial Affidavit filed in March 2025. The father took no responsibility for his nondisclosure, attributing it to his lawyers or asserting the proceedings were irrelevant because the claim concerned “work”.
Medical reports filed in the County Court and tendered before me – including Dr DD’s assessment in mid-2024 – described the father as highly distressed, traumatised, anxious, and depressed, with significant ongoing symptoms. To Dr DD the father described feeling “very anxious, nervous and worried” and that he “becomes very frustrated and irritable”, rating his average mood state “2 out of 10”. The report was clearly not confined to the father’s feelings regarding his career and work. The father told Dr DD he was taking Venlafaxine. Dr DD said the father told him “if he remains in his ‘little bubble at home’ and if ‘nothing out of left field’ happens, then he is able to cope a little better”.
None of this was disclosed to Dr Jones. Notwithstanding, in his oral evidence the father maintained he had been open, transparent and candid with Dr Jones. He also said what he deposed in mid-2024 in his County Court affidavit was true “at the time”, but his situation had significantly improved since he initiated the County Court proceedings.
In January 2025, the father’s treating psychiatrist advised another court that the father was then at significant risk of suicide if he had to return to City B for a hearing there. The father did not disclose this to the Family Report writer when interviewed days later, instead stating he was “the happiest I’ve ever been”.
In February 2025 the father participated in a case conference with EE Company. He described he struggled when considering returning to employment, that he struggled with social requirements, that he had difficulty with focus and that his panic attacks were easily triggered.
A further affidavit sworn by the father in mid-2025 in support of his County Court proceedings continued to describe significant psychiatric symptoms inconsistent with the father’s assertion of a far more positive presentation for the purpose of these proceedings. In his 2025 County Court affidavit, the father described himself as experiencing daily depression, feelings of worthlessness, severe anxiety, panic attacks social avoidance, ongoing sleep disturbances, exhaustion, and reduced memory and concentration. He deposed that although he did not have a plan to self-harm he often felt it would be easier “if I was not here at all”.
This is in stark contrast to the father’s evidence in his trial affidavit, filed earlier in which he made no reference to these apparently chronic and significant issues. He deposed only that following his suicide attempt he had adopted healthier habits and was otherwise in good health. In a subsequent affidavit filed in these proceeding on 14 April 2025 the father maintained his mental health was the best it had been since separation.
The father has alternatively attributed his poor mental health substantially to workplace bullying (in the County Court) or substantially to the mother’s alleged family violence (in these proceedings). He was plainly prepared either to exaggerate symptoms in one context or minimise them in another to achieve his preferred outcome.
The father’s attempts to draw a distinction between his psychological functioning for the purposes of these parenting proceedings, and his psychological functioning for the purposes of his County Court damages application are not tenable. The impairments he asserted in the County Court – sleep disturbance, impaired concentration, limited daily functioning, and suicidality – are plainly relevant to his parenting capacity. Similarly, that his mental health had deteriorated to the point that travelling to City B in early 2025 could place him at significant risk of suicide is relevant to these proceedings.
Further concerns arise from the father uploading false testimonials online to support his now defunct business; his exaggeration to the Child Support Agency (telling them he had correspondence from previous employers of the mother that corroborated his complaints and then admitting to this Court he did not have such correspondence); inaccurate evidence about medication compliance (having asserted strict adherence but later conceding periods of non‑compliance); significant overstatement of the mother’s past injury (deposing she had been hospitalised for at least three months, as opposed to nine days); and denying he concealed job applications from her (when his own witness confirmed that assertion). He also made dramatic but baseless allegations, including that the mother might kill the children or had caused their injuries (including asserting that she had broken Y’s fingers, which was an injury sustained at gymnastics).
The father was not forthcoming regarding the Family Violence Intervention Order obtained by his first wife, Ms FF. She sought an order in 2023 when the father commenced contacting her and continued to do so despite her giving a clear indication she did not want to engage. According to the Magellan report Ms FF asserted their relationship caused her “significant trauma” and ended “under difficult circumstances”. Conversely, to Dr Vergara the father asserted his relationship with Ms FF was not problematic. At trial the father described Ms FF’s application as “very suspect”, that it was “a witch hunt”, “all lies” and “completely fabricated”. He said he could not recall contacting her.
By contrast, the mother’s evidence was generally genuine and considered. However, she appears unable to contemplate alternative explanations for the children’s statements and holds a strong and fixed belief that the children have been sexually abused by their father, even when some of the children’s accounts were plainly implausible. Her evidence was that whilst some statements seemed fanciful, she believed the children were trying to describe real events but lacked the ability to do so accurately. She was able to concede that at times she had asked the children many questions and at times leading questions. She said she wanted to understand what had happened to the children, and to assist them.
None of these observations means that I accept the allegations made by the mother that the father has sexually abused the children and reject his denials.

HOW ARE THE CHILDREN’S BEST INTERESTS ASSESSED?

Section 60CA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) set out that when deciding whether to make a particular parenting order in relation to children, the best interests of the children must be my paramount consideration.
The objects of Part VII of the Act inform how I must exercise my discretion. Those objects include ensuring that the best interests of children are met, including by ensuring their safety (s 60B(a)). The legislation further provides that I must also give effect to the 1989 Convention on the Rights of the Child (s 60B(b)).

THE GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS

There are a range of considerations set out in ss 60CC(2) and (2A) of the Act that I must take into account in determining what is in the child’s best interests.
Relevantly, these sections set out a non-hierarchical list of considerations which include:
(a) consideration of how the child’s safety, and that of those caring for the child can be promoted;

(b) the child’s views;

(c) the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child;

(d) the capacity of those who are proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s needs;

(e) the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with their parents and others significant to the child, where it is safe;

(f) any history of violence, abuse or neglect involving the child and anyone who has been involved in caring for the child; and

(g) whether there have been any family violence orders that have applied to the child or a member of their family; and

(h) anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child.

Promoting safety

I must consider what arrangements would promote the safety of the children and those who have the care of the children, including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm. Both parents alleged there are serious safety concerns facing the children in the care of the other parent.
It was the mother’s case that whilst the Court cannot make a positive finding that the father has sexually abused any of the children, he still presents an unacceptable risk that cannot be ameliorated. Additionally, it was submitted that any orders that require the children to resume a relationship with their father would impact on the mother’s parenting capacity to the children’s detriment. Further, it was her evidence that the father subjected her to coercive controlling behaviours that she said eroded her confidence, and left her feeling incapable, inadequate and insecure.
It was also the mother’s case that the father’s primary proposal for an immediate change of residence demonstrated a poor understanding of the children’s needs – and would subject the children to considerable distress, confusion and dislocation, and leave them feeling unsafe and vulnerable.
Conversely, the father asserted that the mother subjected him to physical violence and coercive control. It was his case that the mother poses a significant risk to the children’s emotional and physical safety. He said their psychological wellbeing is at risk whilst they remain separated from him, and in a household that promotes a false narrative that they have been sexually abused by him. The father asserted the children were also at risk of physical harm in the mother’s care. Under cross examination the father said the children have “always feared their mother and need my help”. He expressed a view that the mother may deliberately harm the children – and could potentially kill them.

Family violence and coercive control

The father asserted the mother perpetrated physical, psychological and verbal abuse throughout the relationship, including incidents between 2014 and 2017 in which he said she struck him, threw objects and verbally abused him and the children. The father first sought to report those allegations to police in June 2023.
The mother acknowledged that the parties argued and at times raised their voices at each other and called each other names. She otherwise denied all such conduct and instead asserted that she was the victim of the father’s psychological abuse and coercive control. She said he was often critical of her appearance, spoke negatively about her weight, ridiculed her outfits, criticised her cooking and threw out her clothes and shoes on two occasions. She described feeling increasingly diminished, hurt and humiliated by his behaviour.
The mother also said the father frequently lied about minor matters, including where he had been, what he had eaten or whether he had applied for a job or not and when confronted, claimed he was “worthless” or “hopeless”. She said he left her notes and sent her text messages suggesting he might self-harm including threatening to gas himself in his car. At times he displayed what seemed to be self-inflicted marks or scratches on his wrists.
When in R Recovery Centre the mother said the father sent her messages implying he wished his attempted suicide had been successful, and other messages asserting he had chest pain, although staff observed no such symptoms. She also described an incident on 7 November 2022 when the father sent messages from Region GG that he was “struggling” and not coping, prompting her to contact his father out of concern for his wellbeing.
These behaviours were substantially denied by the father.
Some contemporaneous material supports aspects of the mother’s evidence. In May 2021 she reported to Ms D that the father criticised her appearance, cooking and housekeeping. At that time Ms D noted the mother appeared to have very low confidence and self-worth. The father’s colleague, Mr F, said he clearly recalled the father advising him he had deliberately removed all traces of job applications he had made so the mother would not know – an account the father could not explain at trial as he said he did not recall telling Mr F this. The father told Dr O in August 2021 that he had a plan to “gas self”, which at trial the father said he could not recall having said. The records from R Recovery Centre confirm that the chest-pain symptoms he reported to the mother were not clinically observed. Dr Jones suggested that this was potentially indicative of the father being deliberately manipulative. The father’s explanation for his messages sent from Region GG – that he found the place therapeutic and calming – was inconsistent with the messages themselves. At trial the father said he could not recall having sent those text messages to the mother.
By contrast there is little independent corroboration for the father’s allegations against the mother. In a recording made by the mother, X described only that the mother had yelled or screamed at the father and “sometimes” spoke to him in a “strong, firm and really loud voice”. None of the children otherwise referred to the mother’s alleged conduct towards the father.
The father’s friends, Mr F, Ms J and Ms G provided little persuasive corroborative evidence. Part of their evidence was based on reports by the father rather than events they had witnessed. Their evidence was also at times generalised and contained broad unparticularised statements. Some of the events described cannot properly be characterised as family violence or coercive control.
Mr F’s affidavit stated that on one occasion the mother requested the father leave work and deliver her a coffee. He said that the father had to check with the mother before applying for other jobs, and if the father wanted to buy a car this was “subject to [Ms Frendo]’s approval” which apparently Mr F found “bizarre”. Mr F also said the mother called the father “several times a day”. I do not regard this evidence as corroborative of the father’s allegations of coercive control or psychological abuse.
I place little weight on the evidence of Ms J. She deposed the father and children “suffered family violence at the hands of [the mother]” based in part on “years of witnessing [the father’s] exposure to family violence” by the mother, and on Y’s “broken arm/hand”. Ms J’s evidence provided little particularisation of any behaviours or events and made broad, generalised statements. As already observed Y’s injury was sustained at gymnastics.
The evidence of Ms G was that the mother telephoned the father frequently and that she seemed excessively needy. I do not regard that as corroborating the father’s evidence that the mother subjected him to coercive control. I put no weight on her evidence.
The father deposed that he saw Mr HH a psychologist from 2020 onwards and that his notes would corroborate the mother’s abusive behaviour. The father maintained he did tell Mr HH about the specific events of violence alleged although it was put to him that Mr HH’s notes contained no references to those specific events. Mr HH did not give evidence to confirm the father’s evidence, and no notes of Mr HH were tendered. A report of Mr HH dated 5 April 2023 was tendered. That referred only to the father having presented in 2020 with a number of stressors, one of which was “relationship discord”.
In several medical and psychological reports between 2020 and 2024 the father does not appear to have disclosed a history of violence by the mother. A note dated 23 January 2020 refers only to the father’s description that the relationship deteriorated in the last year and that the father felt overly defensive and reactive. His description to Dr DD in mid-2024 portrayed the relationship as deteriorating only from about 2022 and there was no reference to the father having been subjected to family violence. In his oral evidence the father was asked why he did not tell Dr DD the allegations of abuse and he said he did not know whether it “came up or not”. The father’s first police report was made in July 2023 and related to a single alleged incident in June 2016.
At trial the father asserted the mother’s behaviour towards his was “already bad” by 2015, but it “got worse” and she became “extremely violent” after X was born. He asserted he was scared of her and attacked on a daily basis. It is difficult to reconcile this evidence with the father’s failure to advise medical professionals – including well past separation – of the allegations and his reports to them that his relationship with the mother deteriorated from about 2019 or later.
For these reasons, together with those already outlined regarding concerns about the father’s veracity, I accept the mother’s evidence as to family violence and coercive control and reject the father’s evidence.
Post separation the father has continued to engage in behaviours that would likely intimidate the mother. In several applications to Child Support the father made serious and inflammatory accusations against her, describing her as deeply dishonest, selfish and abusive. In the application made in February 2024 the father asserted the mother’s “extreme abuse” had prompted his attempted suicide. This is inconsistent with the narrative he provided to the County Court. At trial the father acknowledged this was “an exaggeration” and said he “was emotional”.
In further correspondence with Child Support, the father described that the mother had “done everything she can to destroy me and my parents financially”. Similarly, in his application to vary Child Support made on about 17 April 2024, the father described the mother as causing him extreme financial hardship, as a result of her “fabrications and lies”. He wrote she had caused him to lose his career as a result of her “outrageous and unbelievable accusations”, that she had a “long history of accusing people of outrageous things”, that she is a “narcissist and disgusting human being”, and that “she obviously doesn’t prioritise [the children’s] education”. In a further application dated 26 August 2024 the father said the mother engaged in “disgusting and almost criminal actions” and “unethical exploitation of the legal system”.
I do not accept the father’s evidence that he did not know that the applications would be provided to the mother. At the top of each page of the Child Support document he completed it says “Important: A copy of this page and any supporting evidence will be given to the other party”.
These communications caused the mother significant distress. I agree with the Family Report writer that such communications would likely have increased the mother’s feelings of anxiety and vulnerability.
In May 2024 the father also established a fundraising page linked to his Facebook account, asserting he had been “falsely accused of horrendous acts against my children” and that the legal system discriminated against fathers. He subsequently pleaded guilty to breaching the Intervention Order as a result of this post. The mother described being distressed and concerned about the potential community reaction to the post and the impact it may have on the children.
Additionally, the father adduced evidence from Ms J who suggested concerns for the children’s safety in the mother’s care, based on an injury sustained by Y. This concern was unfounded; Y fractured his finger at gymnastics and received appropriate treatment. There was no basis for Ms J to have communicated with a local MP asserting that Y’s injury was a “really scary urgent Child Protection matter”.
It would be upsetting to the mother to feel under scrutiny and surveillance by friends of the father.

Allegations of sexual abuse

I adopt the important observations of Carew J in Merritt v Merritt [2018] FamCA 1107 at [42]. Allegations of sexual abuse require careful scrutiny: sexual abuse of a child is a grave crime; a loving relationship does not rule out the possibility of abuse; an allegation made in conflict is not automatically dismissed; and the absence of medical evidence does not mean abuse did not occur. Cases involving young children are particularly difficult because adults may misinterpret statements and attempts to clarify those statements may distort them. The consequences of error are severe: either a child is exposed to an abuser, or a child wrongly believes a trusted parent has abused them.
There is no distinct category of “sexual abuse cases”. This remains a parenting matter governed by the best-interests principle. I am not required to determine the allegations with the same precision as a criminal court. As the Full Court observed in Pickford v Pickford [2024] FedCFamC1A 249 at [87], courts should avoid unnecessary factual findings unless they genuinely influence the outcome. The High Court has likewise cautioned against unnecessary findings in risk assessment: M v M (1988) 166 CLR 69 at [76]-[77]; see also Eastley & Eastley (2022) FLC 94–094 at [18], [31].
Accordingly, the question I must determine is whether the father presents an unacceptable risk to the children. As articulated by Fogarty J in N & S & the Separate Representative (1996) FLC 92–655 at [82], [713]-[714], this requires real and substantial consideration of the facts and a reasoned explanation of why the facts do or do not establish such a risk.
The Full Court in Isles & Nelissen [2022] FedCFamC1A 97; (2022) 65 Fam LR 288 (‘Isles & Nelissen’) clarified the required two-step inquiry:
(a) first, assess the allegations and determine, on the balance of probabilities, whether the abuse occurred; and

(b) second, assess whether there is an unacceptable future risk. This involves locating the risk on the spectrum from remotely possible to highly probable, and determining whether it is “so potent it cannot be tolerated”; Austin J in Fitzwater & Fitzwater (2019) 60 Fam LR 212 (‘Fitzwater & Fitzwater’) at [139].

These are two separate enquiries. That second step is predictive. I am required to reach an evidence based conclusion that will inform my overall determination of what is in the children’s best interests.
As noted earlier, counsel for the mother properly conceded that, given the seriousness of the allegations and the contamination arising from the mother’s repeated questioning and the children’s multiple C Mental Health Services appointments, the Court could not be satisfied on the balance of probabilities that the father sexually abused the children. There can be no “comfortable satisfaction” of the alleged facts: Briginshaw v Briginshaw per Rich J.
However, that is not the end of my enquiry; that a positive finding abuse occurred in the past cannot be made does not automatically mean there is no unacceptable risk to the children moving forward. A failure by the mother to adduce evidence that can persuade me that the abuse probably happened is not the same as me being persuaded there is no risk to the children of an unacceptable level. As put by Austin J in Fitzwater & Fitzwater at [138] risk assessment is inherently predictive, requiring the contemplation of possible harm, and risks of harm may warrant consideration, even when improbable.
The mother’s case is that the evidence nonetheless demonstrates a risk posed by the father which is unacceptably high and cannot be mitigated. I must therefore weigh the evidence with a focus on future risks, making a prediction based on the known facts and circumstances: Isles & Nelissen.

Assessment of future risk of sexual harm

Counsel for the mother submitted that, taken cumulatively, the evidence supports a conclusion that the father poses an unacceptable risk of sexual harm to the children. It was argued that even a relatively small risk would still be unacceptable, given the gravity of the potential harm and when considered alongside other concerns about the father.
Having considered the evidence as a whole, I do not agree with that assessment. I am not persuaded that the father presents a risk of sexual harm to the children that rises to the level of “unacceptable.” The possibility of future abuse falls at the “remotely possible” end of the continuum rather than towards probability.

The early allegations and the mother’s questioning

Z’s initial statements referred to his uncle. The mother engaged in repeated conversations with him, during which many of his responses were vague, ambiguous, or plainly fanciful. Dr Vergara considered that following Z’s early statements, an ambient atmosphere of distrust and fear developed in the household around the issue of sexual safety, which may have shaped how family members interpreted the children’s comments and behaviours.
Both parents were concerned when Z made his first statements and sought assistance from SOCIT and C Mental Health Services. Z denied the allegations when interviewed by SOCIT in late August 2022. However, the mother continued to question Z extensively throughout September 2022, including asking whether the alleged person was “a man or a lady” even though all of Z’s statements to that point had concerned his uncle. His change of answer at that time is equally consistent with him attempting to give his mother the “right” answer following repeated questioning. Dr Vergara regarded this type of questioning as leading, particularly because it excluded possibilities other than an adult male or female.
The mother’s evidence was that the children “continued to say numerous things” about what the father had allegedly done. She took notes of their comments, collected drawings, and made extensive video and audio recordings. Many of the behaviours or statements she recorded had no obvious connection to sexual abuse and were capable of innocent explanation.
The mother agreed that at times she questioned one child in the presence of the other children. She agreed she had read books about ‘being safe’ and good and bad touching to the children. There is evidence the mother praised the children for making statements that suggested sexual abuse. The mother also agreed that most of the discussions in which the children made statements were as she was putting them to bed.
The pattern of questioning is significant. The children were often engaged in conversations late at night, often at the mother’s instigation, and often accompanied by subtle rewards – extra attention, time out of bed, or drawing materials. In one recording, X stated she “did not feel like going to bed,” and on another occasion repeatedly asked her mother to continue asking questions, saying detailed questions were easier to answer.

Context of later statements

The children’s first statements suggesting sexual abuse by the father to someone other than the mother were made after months of repeated questioning at home and months of thematic counselling. Z and Y made such statements in mid-2023; X in September 2023.
By that time the children had:
repeatedly discussed the allegations of their father touching them with their mother;
undergone C Mental Health Services counselling focused heavily on concepts of “bad touching,” secrecy, and “being brave” in telling adults;
been exposed to numerous body/sexual safety books; and
participated in SOCIT and Child Protection interviews.
One explanation is that the children eventually felt more comfortable disclosing genuine experiences. The alternative is that the children were primed over time to reinterpret benign events – or to fabricate or confabulate events – because of repeated questioning, thematic counselling, and the rewards associated with making statements consistent with abuse.
Dr Vergara noted that repeated and intense questioning increases the error rate in children’s accounts. She explained that for children of these ages, memories are highly suggestible, and repeated questioning across multiple settings may create pressure to provide consistent narratives. In her oral evidence she expressed concern that the specific literature used with the children may have introduced ideas or frameworks that shaped later statements.
The Family Report writer made similar observations regarding the excessive questioning of children by multiple adults which can confuse the children, distort memories and create false narratives.

Quality and consistency of the children’s statement

The children’s statements were often vague, lacked contextual detail, and in many instances were inconsistent. Examples included references to the father “touching bottoms,” unparticularised “secrets,” and Z’s statement about seeing a penis “make soup.”
X’s accounts were at times marked by uncertainty. She often said she “could not remember,” that she “thought” certain things happened, and that she was unsure whether she actually saw an event or just thought she had.
Y similarly answered, “don’t know” and “don’t remember” to many questions at the first VARE and when first interviewed by Child Protection. Y twice asked his mother how he could find out “if something is a dream or not”. Y reported in one conversation that X was touching Z’s private parts, but then said that he got mixed up and that X had been touching the father’s private parts. He then corrected himself again and said he meant to say the father was touching X’s private parts.
There appear to be plausible alternative explanations for many of the children’s statements and behaviours. This includes that the children being silly and mucking around; such as when there is reference to the children and their father playing around in underwear, laughing and touching each other’s bottoms.
Z’s statements often included plainly fantastical elements: a pear being inserted into his bottom, Santa threatening to eat his penis, monsters and robots entering his room, “baboon parties,” and people in masks making a mess. The mother nonetheless maintained that the children were attempting to disclose something real but could not articulate it, and she appeared unable to contemplate that the children may have invented aspects of their accounts. Dr Jones was critical in his evidence that the mother “lacked a forensic posture” around the children’s disclosures.
There were inconsistencies between the statements made by Y and X including:
whether the father touched genitalia. For instance, X said he touched her bottom and did not touch the children’s genital. Y said the father had touched X’s vagina and the boys’ genitals;
what they witnessed. For instance, X said the father touched her on the bottom when they were lying in bed. Y said he saw the father put his finger in X’s bottom when they were standing up; and
where events occurred. For instance, Y said he was with X at the door to the bathroom when the father put his finger in Z’s bottom. X said she saw this when the father and Z were on the lounge.
It is notable that the allegations evolved and escalated over time, from somewhat vague references to touching to increasingly severe acts including alleged oral and anal penetration. The children did not report a number of matters to SOCIT or Child Protection including that the father had put his penis in Y’s mouth, or in Y’s bottom or that he had bitten Z’s penis.

Adult interpretation of behaviours

There is a real risk that the mother has interpreted ordinary childhood behaviours as evidence of abuse. Dr Vergara cautioned that many behaviours described as being potential indicators of abuse could equally be self-soothing, exploratory, or developmentally normal. She noted that parental fear can significantly influence interpretation. C Mental Health Services at times appeared to encourage the mother to treat behaviours as disclosures rather than neutrally assessing them.
A number of the statements made by the children appear to have been interpreted by the adults as confirmation of sexual abuse. For instance, X’s statement that it felt like a forearm was wiped against her bottom has been interpreted to be potentially referring to an erect penis. The references to “honey poo” and there being something ‘sticky’ on the floor have been interpreted as a potential reference to semen. These descriptions might be consistent with sexual activity. However, the children may have been describing something else altogether.
Certain reports were difficult to reconcile with sexual abuse, such as Y’s reluctance to show his stomach, references to watching YouTube with the father, Y drawing a picture with many family members but omitting the father, and Z spinning around on his bottom and making silly noises and saying it was a secret. The mother nonetheless viewed these matters through the lens of her belief that abuse had occurred.
The mother’s belief that the children had been sexually abused was strongly encouraged by both SOCIT and C Mental Health Services. During a discussion with the mother in August 2022, the police officer repeatedly told her that “you have to believe what the kids say”, that many people only report abuse years later “because people that love them the most and are meant to protect them did not believe them”, and that “the most important thing is believing him because if it comes out later that you didn’t, that lives on”.
The documents tendered from C Mental Health Services make it plain they formed a view the children were disclosing sexual abuse. Ms V’s May 2024 report summarised the notes and sessions in their file. That – and similar – reports from C Mental Health Services referred to a number of behaviours allegedly exhibited by the children – including bed wetting, night terrors, loss of appetite, separation anxiety and aggression. Dr Vergara noted that according to C Mental Health Services “all of [these behaviours] were attributed to molestation as opposed to other factors, such as parental separation or change in living arrangements”. That is, C Mental Health Services did not seem to consider alternate explanations for the children’s behaviours, other than as confirming sexual abuse.
In addition, X’s psychologist, Ms E, appeared to have formed a view that X had been abused despite X never making a disclosure to her. E suggested X’s reluctance to sleep in her bedroom could indicate night-time abuse, and she stated she would not be an appropriate psychologist for X if the Court ultimately rejected the allegations.

Dr Vergara

Dr Vergara conducted a psychosexual assessment of the father. While she identified some general risk markers relating to mental health history, coping strategies and past alcohol use, she concluded these were not associated with sexual offending risk. She assessed the father as presenting a low risk of future sexual violence and noted no evidence of sexual deviance, prior concerning behaviour, or sexual interest in children.
At trial Dr Vergara confirmed she had not been aware of the father’s application to the County Court, including the contents of the affidavit sworn in those proceedings two months after seeing her. She said the inconsistencies between what the father reported to her, and what he deposed to in the County Court proceedings did not alter her assessment of the father, which was based on broader data.
In her evidence, Dr Vergara proposed an alternative explanation for the children’s allegations, suggesting that the breakdown of the parental relationship and the highly emotional environment may have led to ordinary behaviours being misinterpreted or sexualised. She also noted that C Mental Health Services encouraged the mother to believe the children were disclosing abuse, rather than adopting a neutral or investigative stance – an issue also highlighted by Dr Jones, who referred to the service’s overly definitive language.
Dr Vergara emphasised the developmental stage of the children at the time: young children can provide vague or incomplete accounts, are prone to confabulation (filling memory gaps with invented details), and may easily misinterpret benign actions as inappropriate. Examples were noted, including X reporting non-abusive interactions, such as with peers or when cared for by family.
She further observed that young children are particularly susceptible to the influence of adults and repetition, may blend imagination with reality, and may repeat information they have heard from others. With repeated questioning over time, their accounts may become more elaborate or inconsistent as they attempt to satisfy the expectations of questioners, including their mother or professionals.
These are all important considerations.

Other ameliorating factors

In terms of ameliorating factors, the children are now older and more articulate. They are well aware of bodily boundaries and well educated regarding different kinds of touching. These matters reduce the likelihood they would be at physical risk in their father’s care.

Determination – no unacceptable risk of sexual harm

Having considered all the evidence, I accept the plausibility of Dr Vergara’s alternate hypothesis: that the mother became hypervigilant following Z’s initial statements, that C Mental Health Services reinforced her belief that abuse had occurred, and that the children’s later statements were shaped by repeated questioning and therapeutic contamination rather than reflecting actual events.
I agree with the observation by Dr Jones that the mother lacked a forensic posture and had reinforced certain narratives, possibly in the context of her own emotional processing of the separation. He noted a “biased heuristic” whereby the mother filtered information consistent with her belief that the father had abused the children.
