Her evidence indicated she does not appear to have scrutinised the statements made by the children or considered explanations for them other than the children were disclosing abuse. Rather, with the explicit encouragement of various authorities, she uncritically accepted the children’s statements as disclosures of sexual abuse by their father, and interpreted, and re-interpreted past events, and the children’s behaviours and statements through that lens.
In terms of the predictive exercise I must undertake, it seems that the likelihood of the possibility that the children would be abused by the father in the future is remote. Taking all the evidence and circumstances into account, I am not satisfied that the father presents a risk to the children of sexual harm in the future to the extent that would be an unacceptable risk.
Again, that is not the end of my enquiry – and such a determination does not necessarily result in orders for an immediate change of residence, or for the children to resume spending time with their father. There are many other factors which I must consider in determining the appropriate outcome for these children.

Risk of psychological harm if the children remain in the mother’s care

It was the submission by counsel for the father that there is an unacceptable risk of harm to the children’s psychological wellbeing if they remain in their mother’s care where they will continue to falsely believe they have been the victims of sexual abuse perpetrated by their father when that has not occurred.
As already set out, I am not satisfied on the balance of probabilities that the father has sexually abused the children as asserted.
The harm to the children that arises if they wrongly believe they have been abused is obviously significant. They will believe an inaccurate and destructive narrative about their father, their childhood and themselves. This will likely impact their emotional lives, their development, their sense of self and their view of the world and their place in it. As noted by the Family Report writer if they believe they have been abused, they will experience a whole range of trauma responses and long-term emotional difficulties similar to those experienced by actual victims of abuse. This could include issues with sleeping, separation issues, peer relationship difficulties, impacts on ability to engage at school, lower self-esteem, and struggling with a sense of belonging. The Family Report writer also opined the children would likely reflect on their childhood later in life and would be likely to feel considerable confusion and emotional trauma.
The children remain separated from their father, and their extended paternal family. These matters raise significant risks of harm.
It is troubling that the mother has engaged in repeated conversations with the children about the allegations of abuse. This was at times at her instigation, when she sought “clarification” about alleged events. At times, she persevered with questioning the children over their objections. Many of the conversations took place at bedtime – with likely unintended rewards for the child. I have already referred to the difficulties with leading and repeated questions and the likelihood that memories become contaminated. It can also create the consolidation of inaccurate memories.
Given my findings as to the allegations, and my assessment of risk, it is hoped the mother will cease having any such conversations with the children. If she continues to do so, it is likely this will cause the children further harm. Similarly, further counselling with C Mental Health Services will likely further cement the children’s belief that their father sexually abused them notwithstanding that given the matters outlined, the reliability of the children’s statements is in serious doubt.

Additional safety concerns

The mother’s functioning

Having assessed that the father does not pose an unacceptable risk of sexual harm to the children, I must also consider the mother’s belief in the occurrence of the abuse and the impact that may have on her parenting capacity if the children are to be reintroduced to their father.
It was plain that the mother deeply and genuinely believes all three children have been sexually abused by their father and that they would be at significant risk if he was to resume a role in their lives.
Given this strongly and genuinely held belief, I am concerned as to the impact on the mother’s emotional and psychological functioning in the event the children were to be reintroduced to their father. I anticipate orders that require the children to be reintroduced to their father would be very difficult for the mother to manage. These matters are relevant to questions of the mother’s safety and that of the children, in the event her parenting becomes significantly compromised to the extent the children’s wellbeing is adversely affected. I will return to this issue shortly when considering the mother’s capacity.

The impact on the children if removed from the mother

I share the Family Report writer’s serious concerns about the likely impact on the children’s emotional wellbeing if they were immediately placed with the father.
The Family Report writer observed that, as at January 2025, the children were entrenched within the maternal system, and the abuse narratives were firmly established. Even if the Court ultimately found the allegations did not meet the threshold for unacceptable risk her evidence was that compelling the children to spend time with the father may be problematic and would likely require therapeutic support from a skilled professional. She said orders that required the children’s relocation to Queensland would significantly disrupt the children’s sense of security, stability and the support structures on which they rely. She expected a change of residence “may very likely destabilise the children and cause great distress”, that it would be “very, very difficult” for the children and generate “a high degree of confusion”.
I accept that the psychological risk associated with abruptly removing the children from their mother, relocating them interstate to a new household, schools, community and support systems, and limiting their time with their mother to supervised contact, is significant. I agree with the Family Report writer’s assessment that this risk is heightened given that, for more than two years, the children have viewed the father as unsafe and have relied on the mother and the maternal family as their source of comfort and security.
I do not accept that the children would likely transition smoothly into the father’s care, or that any distress would be short-lived. They have lived with their mother for their entire lives, have no relationship with the father or his wife and her children, and have established longstanding ties to City B, including school, friends and extracurricular activities. X has a history of separation anxiety and school refusal and has been engaged with the same psychologist since 2021. All of these familiar supports would be severed.
As the Family Report writer asked; how do the children reconcile all that has happened to them and how do they make sense of who is safe in their lives? Given the complex history it seems unrealistic to expect that removing the children from everything that is familiar and safe could be anything other than deeply distressing and confusing. Even requiring them to participate in family therapy would likely be highly challenging. The Family Report writer observed that currently “any contact is likely to be destabilising and potentially distressing”. As counsel for the Independent Children's Lawyer said, it is difficult to see how the children can be persuaded that they are safe with their father.

The father’s functioning

I have already referred to the father’s chronic mental health issues and the inconsistent evidence the father presented about his functioning in different settings. As set out later in these reasons, I remain concerned as to his capacity to appropriately care for the children and to respond to and manage their distress and confusion in the event they were placed in his care, and as to his ability to shield the children from his views of the mother. I am also concerned about the impacts on the children’s emotional and psychological functioning and development if they were in their father’s care when his mental health is poor.

Views expressed by the children

I have limited evidence as to the views of the children.
Neither Y nor X expressed a view regarding seeing their father to the Family Report writer. Z was not interviewed.
The Independent Children's Lawyer was recently appointed and had not interviewed the children. Her predecessor had done so. I was not advised that the children had expressed any views. The mother said that after meeting with the Independent Children's Lawyer in April 2025, X became panicked about potentially spending time with the father.
The mother said X believes her father “touched” her “private parts which shouldn’t have happened”. She said the boys have the same belief. She said they accept it would not be safe to see him.
There is some evidence that in August 2023 X expressed a positive view of her father. I have referred to the Father’s Day poster X created at school about which she was excited. In the poster she had written about the ways she loved her father. As already observed, in response to this poster C Mental Health Services discussed with X her “two different experiences” of her father, being the “hero” and the one that did “bad touching”. Subsequently X told C Mental Health Services she wanted to see her father, if he did not keep “touching” her. In October 2023 X told C Mental Health Services that she wanted her father to come home, but not to do “bad touching”.
The mother reported in mid-October 2023 to C Mental Health Services that X became fearful of a man at a fast food outlet who “reminded” her of the father. In November 2023 X told the counsellor at C Mental Health Services she was scared of her father being angry with her.
In May 2024 X told Child Protection the father “is not a safe person”. Ms E said X “understands” that the father “has done bad things to her” and she does not want to see him. X told the Family Report writer that the father did not live with her because “he did bad things”.
In September and again in October 2022 Y had told the mother that he wanted his father. In late September 2023 the mother advised C Mental Health Services that she had been reading a “social story” to X and Y, and at the part when it referred to speaking to a police officer she reported Y burst into tears and said “but I want to see Daddy”. The mother told C Mental Health Services that in that conversation, X asked if the father “was a bad man”. The mother said her response was he was not a bad man and had done a lot of good things to the children “but that the touching secrets were not okay”.
By 2024, Y’s view of the father, as told to Child Protection was that he was “not a safe person”. When asked by the Family Report writer about his family or his relationship, Y “became quickly withdrawn and non-communicative”.
It is difficult to see how X and Y might be positive about seeing their father given the adults’ responses and messaging they received when expressing that they wanted to see him.
Z told C Mental Health Services in October 2023 that his father was “bad”. The mother said Z told her on 14 March 2025 that he was scared his father had attended the school sports day, and that when he gets scared about the father “his whole body goes funny”. She said Z became visibly distressed and cried at school that day and continued to be distressed and complained of a sore stomach for a few days. She said Z said on 19 March 2025 that he was “scared about Daddy”.
The Family Report writer noted that the children have “for over two years” regarded their father as “unsafe” and that they believe they have been harmed by him. She said the father “does not seem to feature in the children’s lives or their home, other than the knowledge that he is not safe, and that is why is [sic] no longer part of their lives”. She noted that over the previous years any discussions regarding the father appear to have “focussed on disclosures of sexual harm, fears and safety”.

Developmental, psychological, emotional, and cultural needs of the children

The children have complex psychological needs. I accept they believe they have been sexually abused by their father – or at least that he has ‘done bad things’ to them.
I note the observations by the Family Report writer that the children:
...present as delightful well cared for children. They present commensurate to their ages and developmental stages, with no reported developmental, neuroatypical disorders, or learning delays. They demonstrated a good vocabulary, age appropriate, able to be understood, polite, engaging, and chatty when playing or discussing activities and other child related or child focussed issues. They impressed as more naive children, on the younger side of their developmental years, somewhat sheltered and it may be that their social relational or emotional development is somewhat younger than their years.
At trial, X was described as experiencing a lot of “separation anxiety” from at least 2021. It is apparent the mother’s responses had some impact on X’s ability to separate. This is reflected in the notes of Ms D who noted in February 2021 that she discussed with the mother the “importance of showing confidence in self and in [X] and strategies for management of her own coping and anxiety”.
Z has issues with food and eating. He attends an allied health professional. The mother obtained a referral to CAMHS, who advised the mother they were considering referring Z back to C Mental Health Services.
The mother conceded she has at times experienced difficulties with ensuring the children’s regular attendance at school. She said since moving out of the former matrimonial home in about August 2024 school attendance has improved. The mother deposed she regularly meets with the children’s teachers and wellbeing leaders to ensure she is doing all she can to support the children’s school attendance.

Capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for their developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs

The Family Report writer noted that each parent was capable of providing for the children’s everyday needs. They both are aware of childhood development and children’s needs for routine, stability and structure. Both parents were able to refer to the children’s personalities and styles.

The mother

The Family Report writer described the mother as having demonstrated “commitment and dedication” to the children, and “a strong child focus” though she appeared hypervigilant regarding potential abuse. Those observations accord with my own.
In her psychological assessment, Dr Jones noted some validity anomalies due to the mother minimising shortcomings, though her scores remained valid and her overall profile was within normal limits. He described her interpersonal style as “submissive, conforming, and perhaps naïve”, prone to feeling overwhelmed under mild pressure, somewhat dependent and anxiously predisposed. He considered her parenting style passive and overly accommodating, at times affecting the children’s school attendance. He found no acute illness or personality disorder, though some Cluster B features were present.
Dr Jones considered the mother was “highly susceptible to a regression” in her mental state and recommended she receive psychological support. He opined that she may have inadvertently reinforced the children’s statements and that any orders providing time with the father would likely heighten her anxiety, co-dependence and fear of abandonment. He expressed concern about her difficulties in getting the children to school, viewing them as manifestations of anxiety and her underlying personality structure. He described the mother as “anxiously pre-disposed”.
In oral evidence, Dr Jones described the mother as anxious, “very worried”, rigid in her thinking and emotionally needy. Her anxious disposition and fixed views about the allegations were evident throughout the hearing.
Although the mother presently meets the children’s needs, given her entrenched views, it is likely that any orders that require the children to resume a relationship with their father would be highly confronting for her. Her counsel submitted that such orders would adversely impact the mother’s capacity to parent to the point it would impinge on the children’s wellbeing.
Ms D, the mother’s treating psychologist since 2021, gave evidence of the mother’s significant anxiety and stress. She reported that any interaction with the father was a source of fear and dread for the mother, and that the mother remained “very fearful” of him.
However, at trial Ms D expressed some confidence that the mother would comply with court orders that required the children to spend time with their father, and that her parenting capacity would not be negatively impacted by such orders. She said – somewhat colloquially – the mother would “not go crazy”.
I am concerned that Ms D’s longstanding therapeutic relationship with the mother may have compromised her impartiality. Dr Jones and Ms K were far less optimistic about the mother’s ability to cope with any orders reintroducing the father.
As already observed, the mother’s belief that the father has perpetrated sexual abuse on all three children is deeply and genuinely held. Dr Jones in his oral evidence acknowledged the children would likely pick up on the mother’s hesitance and worries if they were to spend time with their father, impacting their ability to comfortably transfer from one parent to another. He considered the mother’s ability to shield the children from her views, or to be positive or hopeful to the children if they were to spend time with their father was likely “entirely eroded”. His evidence was that the mother was highly susceptible to a regression in her mental state and that “compromises to [the mother’s] mental state and functioning and outlook... have flow-on effects for her children”. For instance, he expressed a view that the mother’s struggle to get the children to school at times could be a manifestation of when she felt overwhelmed, hopeless and unsupported.
Similarly, the Family Report writer expressed concern that the mother’s parenting capacity could be undermined and diminished with the potential to significantly impact on the children.
I have significant concerns as to the impact on the mother’s parenting if orders are made that provide for the children to be reintroduced to their father. The mother’s evidence is that these proceedings have left her feeling “incredibly stressed, fragile, intimidated, inferior, insecure and anxious”. She deposed her confidence has been “shattered” and she is dealing with “immense grief”. She deposed that she feels “overwhelmed with the magnitude of our situation” at times, and that whilst she endeavours to hide her feelings from the children the stress and anxiety at times becomes “debilitating”.
When asked how she would manage if the children were to spend time with their father the mother described being scared, disappointed and extremely worried. It was an outcome she could not contemplate. She became visibly distressed on multiple occasions during the proceedings when asked about the abuse allegations. At times she became so distressed and overwhelmed that a short adjournment was required.
I anticipate those feelings of fear, stress, overwhelm, grief and anxiety would be exacerbated by any order that required the mother to facilitate the children spending time with or communicating with the father.
I am satisfied there is a real risk that orders requiring the children to be reintroduced to the father would compromise the mother’s mental state and functioning, which in turn would negatively affect the children, and impact their sense of safety and security. It is unlikely she could effectively manage her distress, fear, and worry, or shield the children from the emotional difficulties she would likely experience if required to facilitate time between the children and their father.
Taking all the evidence into account, I am not satisfied the mother acted maliciously in making the allegations. She was encouraged by C Mental Health Services and SOCIT to accept the children’s statements. In those circumstances, maintaining a more forensic attitude to the children’s statements and behaviours may have been difficult.
It is to the mother’s credit that she has attempted to keep the family’s ordeal private, although in doing so she remains somewhat isolated and unsupported.
The mother has otherwise met the children’s needs appropriately. She presented as diligent, attuned to the children’s needs and committed to providing them with a loving and secure home.

The father

The Family Report writer described the father as being focused on the falsity of the abuse allegations, his concern that the mother had alienated the children and attempting to impress as the “more superior parent”. She said the father’s proposals “seemed to lack insight into the impact upon the children especially introducing them to his new family formation”. The father presented similarly at trial.
As already observed, the father’s case was run substantially on the basis that the mother and C Mental Health Services have concocted false abuse allegations and the only viable solution to that is an immediate change of residence. It was the father’s case that the harm to the children of remaining in the mother’s care outweighed any harm to the children if care arrangements were radically altered. However, the Court has concerns as to the father’s capacity to provide appropriate care for the children, which may harm the children’s wellbeing and psychological safety if the children are placed in his care. In particular, the Court is concerned as to the father’s mental health; his ability to prioritise the children’s needs; and his ability to promote the children’s relationship with their mother.

The father’s mental health

The father has a long history of mental health difficulties. He currently sees Dr H (psychiatrist) and Mr KK (psychologist), and previously attended Mr HH (psychologist). He began treatment with Dr H in early 2023, usually via Telehealth.
Medical records show the father experienced a depressive episode about ten years before 2020. At trial the father conceded his mental health “wasn’t good” from mid-2019. In early 2020, medical records confirm he presented with depression and anxiety, reporting occasional suicidal thoughts without intent. In a letter the father wrote about his work injury in March 2021 he described experiencing chronic headaches, migraines, nausea, and periods of being bedridden, sometimes accompanied by thoughts of ending his life. At trial the father said he did not remember having those thoughts prior to September 2021 and said he was “extremely tired” when he wrote that letter. However, in August 2021, he reported to Dr O worsening depression with at least daily suicidal thoughts and plans, and was diagnosed with major depression, significant anxiety, and possible obsessional personality traits. At trial, the father said he did not recall “being at that level”.
At an assessment with Mr LL in December 2022 – shortly after his attempted suicide – the father was diagnosed with an adjustment disorder with predominantly anxious mood.
In January 2023 Dr O described the father’s mental state as reasonably stable. In April 2023 Mr HH observed the father was managing multiple stresses, with no signs of significant deterioration or relapse of his depression.
In August and November 2023, the father told Dr H he was happier than he had been for years. He decided – against medical advice – to stop antidepressants. By mid-November, his mood “crashed,” and he resumed medication. In early 2024, the father reported he was compliant with his medication regime but later admitted reducing his dosage. An assessment by a Dr MM dated 12 February 2024 identified that the father was “vulnerable to relapse under psychosocial stress” and concluded his major depressive disorder with anxious distress was “chronic and will require long-term treatment”. Dr MM understood the father was taking Venlafaxine 225mg.
At some point in 2024 the father again discontinued medication against medical advice.

Dr Jones’ Assessment (interview – 3 June 2024)

Dr Jones interviewed the father and found his psychometric tests invalid, suggesting “positive impression management” and lack of insight. The father claimed he was medication free and “the happiest I’ve ever been,” omitting that he had stopped medication against advice and failing to disclose the County Court proceedings he was in the process of initiating. The father also incorrectly advised Dr Jones that there was no reported history of family violence with previous partners. Dr Jones concluded the father appeared stable and functional at that time but was vulnerable to relapse and had a complex mental health history into which the father perhaps lacked insight. He noted persecutory thinking, resentment toward the mother, and a precarious sense of self. He warned that past depressive episodes and maladaptive coping (e.g. frequent relocations) made future difficulties likely.

Later Deterioration (Late 2024–2025)

By November 2024, Dr H reported a significant decline in the father’s mental health. He presented with low mood, panic attacks near schools or police, and renewed suicidal thoughts after the father again ceased medication. Dr H advised restarting antidepressants. Dr H provided letters to the Magistrates’ Court in late 2024 and early 2025 that warned travel to City B posed severe mental health risk and placed the father at significant risk of self-harm.
In May 2025, Dr H diagnosed the father as having an adjustment disorder, noting the father had remarried and professionally retrained but remained fragile, unable to enter schools or work due to anxiety and panic attacks.
According to Dr H’s notes from the father’s most recent appointment in July 2025, the father had reported inter alia significant struggles over the preceding fortnight including experiencing distress, being tearful, disturbed sleep, memory issues, feelings of paranoia and being overwhelmed. The treatment plan included increasing the antidepressant dosage from 150mg to 225mg daily.

Evidence at trial

Dr Jones maintained that at the time he assessed the father, he appeared to be stable and functional. However, he agreed that if the father’s reports to Dr H were accurate. the father was not – as at July 2025 – able to safely care for the three children, especially given their likely emotional needs if there was a change of residence.
When informed about the father’s reports to Dr H, the Family Report writer also acknowledged that given the children’s likely dysregulation and distress she expected the father would struggle significantly to manage – either if they were placed in his care or if he was to be reintroduced to them via a therapeutic process.
Dr Jones also expressed a view that the persecutory aspects to the father’s thinking (for instance, the father claimed Q Centre was “notorious for accusing men of sexual misconduct in order to have them removed from the home”) which would likely make it difficult for a family therapist to engage the father and could lead to difficulties for those providing any supervision of the children’s time if they sought to impose limits or boundaries.
There was further evidence of the father’s persecutory thinking at trial. For instance, he expressed a view that C Mental Health Services had a “campaign” to create a new “stolen generation” of children estranged from fathers due to false sexual abuse allegations. In his trial affidavit he asserted the mother and C Mental Health Services were “complicit” in their attempts to isolate the children from him and in coaching the children to make disclosures against him. He asserted C Mental Health Services had attempted “to influence the judiciary to grant a no contact IVO against me”.
The father also expressed a view that the children’s school had taken sides, were not appropriately catering for the children and had acted unprofessionally. He said the school had colluded with Q Centre, C Mental Health Services and the police. Additionally, he referred to police being investigated for seeking an Intervention Order against him. He described them as “blindsided by their gender bias”.
I note further that in 2024, when the father’s application to reduce the amount of child support was not successful, he complained he had been “treated with absolute zero respect, courtesy and treated worse than an animal by the narrow minded, judgemental and obviously incompeteant peropel” [sic] at the agency. In that correspondence he referred to the mother being supported by a “deceitful and unprincipled Tax Payer funded organisation”.
Dr H’s evidence at trial was that the father had an adjustment disorder, caused by his workplace but maintained by the family law dispute. He said the father was not permanently disabled and he expected the father to recover.
In his oral evidence, the father said Dr H’s correspondence with the City B Magistrates’ Court was “a bit exaggerated”. The father maintained his mental health at the time of the hearing before me was the “best it’s ever been” and that it had been getting “better and better and better”. He said he could now attend school without difficulties. The father said he distinguished between his psychological functioning as it pertained to work and his psychological functioning as it pertained to parenting as an explanation of the discrepancies between his statements to professionals, and in his evidence to this Court and to the County Court. He also said that his mood went “up and down” and that “things change”.
I do not accept these as satisfactory explanations for the stark differences in the father’s own account of his functioning and wellbeing. The father’s claims to this Court, to the Family Report writer and to Dr Jones that he was functioning well are difficult to reconcile with the severe symptoms he reported to psychiatrists and to the County Court at similar times. It may be that the father has minimised his mental health difficulties in these proceedings and has perhaps been more candid with his treating psychiatrist. This raises concern as to his reliability – as already noted – as to his insight, and as to his mental health functioning.

The father’s ability to prioritise the children’s needs

Dr Jones described the father’s proposal for the children to relocate as “ill-considered,” reflecting poor judgment and a lack of child-focused decision-making. He noted the father showed little understanding of the challenges that would arise if reunification occurred, particularly given the likelihood that the children would reject him after years without contact. Dr Jones also considered the father’s then alternate plan – living between Victoria and City NN and travelling for contact – as “seemingly unsustainable.” In oral evidence, Dr Jones characterised the proposal for an immediate change of residence as “an unserious proposition... that’s not going to work,” raising concerns about the father’s reasoning and ability to prioritise the children’s needs.
Dr Vergara similarly observed the father’s “lack of introspection and unrealistic assessment of the necessary steps and potential implications.” She suggested his expectations appeared driven by a need for validation and vindication rather than the children’s best interests, risking emotional harm and distress to them.
I share these concerns. The father demonstrated limited insight into the negative impact of removing the children from their mother’s care and their lives in City B. At trial, he was dismissive of the evidence given by the experts warning about potential distress, insisting any harm would be “less than” remaining with their mother. He relied on his own research into “alienation,” claiming children’s fear “doesn’t occur” once they live with the “alienated parent.” He asserted that any difficulties could be resolved with “the right strategies,” though he provided no detail of strategies he could employ, beyond naming a clinic near his home as a “one-stop shop” for counselling – without evidence of its services or staff expertise.
The father appeared naïve in assuming the children would be “jumping in the car” to reunite with him. Although he later conceded they might need professional support to encourage them to travel home with him, he suggested this could be achieved within days. He offered no clear plan or strategies for helping the children transition to a new home, school, and family in Queensland, away from their mother and familiar environment.
This lack of preparation raises serious concerns about his ability to manage the children’s emotional needs if placed in his care.

The father’s attitude towards the mother

The father’s persistently negative view of the mother is deeply concerning.
Dr Jones noted that the father described the mother as “deeply disturbed, violent and vengeful.” To Dr Jones, the father claimed the mother suffered a serious head injury, after which she became accusatory and controlling. He further alleged she had borderline personality disorder or narcissism and said he was “petrified of her.” He reported sleeping with knife (described by the father at trial as being a kitchen knife) under his bed out of fear she might attack him and claimed she encouraged him to kill himself. He portrayed her as demanding and jealous, attributing her behaviour to “a combination of her head injury... extreme narcissism or borderline.”
These negative feelings do not appear to have diminished since his interview with Dr Jones.
In his trial affidavit the father asserted the mother sought to cause his “financial and reputational ruin” and that “her claims were a calculated ruse to leverage [City B’s] local resources to ostracise and isolate me”. He said that had prevented him from continuing his career as an educator notwithstanding the documents he filed in the County Court asserted the substantial blame for that lay elsewhere. He said the mother has engaged in a “continued campaign” against him. He deposed that when Y allegedly suffered a broken arm and fractured fingers in November 2023, he believed the mother was responsible. Notwithstanding the gymnastics incident report, the father deposed the mother had caused the injury. At trial the father said he was “on the fence” about the true cause of Y’s injury and did not know what to believe, as he had “seen what she’s capable of”.
I have already referred to the comments he made about the mother to the Child Support agency.
At trial the father maintained that the children were fearful of their mother and described her as having “no limits,” suggesting they were her only focus in life. He maintained the mother should undergo a psychiatric or neuropsychological assessment which would show the children had always feared their mother and needed his help. He appeared resentful and embittered toward her. He also asserted that she might harm or even kill the children, though there was no basis for such allegations.
On several occasions, he stated he was considering suing the mother for compensation over the sexual abuse allegations as there should be “consequences”. When asked about his decision to move interstate, he claimed, “I had no choice,” portraying himself as a victim.
It is difficult to see how the father could support the children to maintain a relationship with their mother or shield them from his negative views if they were in his primary care. I share Dr Jones’ concern that the father’s ability to restrain his hostility toward the mother if the children were to spend time with him, identifying this as “probably the greatest source of risk” to their wellbeing.

The benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with their parents, and other people who are significant to the child where it is safe to do so

In most cases there will be a positive benefit to the children having both parents involved in their lives. However, the Full Court has observed that in some cases orders to attempt to foster a relationship with one parent will be of no positive benefit to a child, and that to make such orders would not be in the child’s best interests: McCall & Clark (2009) FLC 93–405 at [122].
Whilst it may be that the children would benefit from having a relationship with each of their parents, given the concerns I have outlined about the risks to these children, it does not currently appear emotionally or psychologically safe for the children to have a relationship with both parents. Moreover, given the lack of evidence presented at trial as to an appropriate therapeutic pathway, it is also difficult to envisage a way in which the children – and their parents – could be safely supported to resume their relationship with the father.

The history or family violence, abuse or neglect, and any family violence order that applies or has applied

I have already set out at length the evidence and my findings in relation to the parties’ competing allegations of family violence.
The mother obtained a Family Violence Intervention Order against the father in late 2022. An order was made in early 2023 on an interim basis.
The father was charged with several breaches of the Family Violence Intervention Order between late 2022 and early 2023 as a result of his mother delivering presents to the children and him putting photographs of the children on Facebook. I understand he received a diversion order. To Dr Vergara he said after being charged with breaching the order “I then put a cross [Intervention Order] against her”. The father’s application was based on historical allegations of family violence. He was living interstate at the time, and was not having any contact with the children. It appears that application was somewhat retaliatory.
The Family Violence Intervention Order proceedings were resolved in October 2024 with the parties agreeing to mutual undertakings for 12 months. The mother said she agreed to the matter being resolved on that basis as she found the process overwhelming. Those undertakings have now expired.

PARENTING ORDERS TO BE MADE

I have already set out the competing proposals. The Court is not bound to the outcomes agitated by each of the parents. However, the determination I must make as to the orders that will meet the children’s best interests has to be based on the evidence presented to the Court. As already observed, the evidence presented at trial did not identify any realistic or safe pathway for the children to be re-introduced to their father.

Live with

Taking all the evidence into account, I am satisfied the children’s best interests are met by remaining living with their mother. They have lived with her throughout their lives. She anticipates remaining in City B, where the children are in a familiar home, and attending school and participating in extra-curricular activities.
The risks to the children’s wellbeing – the likely emotional distress, confusion and dislocation – of being removed from their mother’s care and placed with their father are significant. Those risks are amplified by the concerns I have as to the father’s functioning; his ability to promote the children’s needs above his own; whether he can sensitively and appropriately assist the children to transition into his care; and if he is able to promote the children’s relationship with their mother. I also have no evidence from the father’s wife and accordingly have no real understanding of her ability to assist and support the father. I do note she attended the final hearing, and was described by the Family Report writer as “engaging, pleasant” and that it was her intention to support the father in whatever he needed to do to assist the children.
That the father lives in Queensland adds another element of difficulty for the children – as a change of residence would require them to leave behind all that is familiar, including schools, their home, their friends and their community.
The father’s proposal for a change of residence was poorly developed and, in practical terms, unworkable. There would likely be practical difficulties with the children being removed from their mother’s care and placed with their father. The father’s evidence suggested he had given limited thought to how the changeover to his care could be safely implemented. Initially he suggested he could drive to Victoria, and the children might be excited to see him. He then suggested he could collect the children from their maternal grandparents’ home or that the parties could utilise a contact centre as he recognised that it could take an extended time over more than one day for the children to transition into his care. He suggested that perhaps the children could live with their grandparents while he re-engaged with them. He said he would ‘get advice from professionals’, and on day three of the final hearing he said he would be “ringing a whole lot of people” that afternoon for advice.
The father provided no detail about schooling or therapeutic supports available in Queensland. His counsel referred only to a “one stop shop” called OO Mental Health Services , which was said to have multidisciplinary services, but no evidence was adduced as to its capacity or expertise.
In the event the children were moved into his care, the father proposed there be a moratorium of time with the mother for between six to eight weeks after relocation, followed by supervised time once a month at PP Contact Centre in Suburb QQ or other supervised service near his home. No evidence was provided about availability or cost. Counsel speculated that assistance could be offered promptly at $25 per hour per parent. There was no suggestion the father would contribute to the substantial travel and accommodation costs the mother would incur in travelling from City B to Suburb QQ. These practical difficulties of the children spending time with their mother if they were to live interstate with their father is another complicating factor.
I note again the father’s evidence that he will not return to live in City B or its surrounds as he does not feel safe. Accordingly, it was not an option to make an order that required the father to return to Victoria.

Time with

Although I have found that the father does not pose an unacceptable risk of sexual abuse, the evidence does not permit the Court to construct any workable or safe regime in which the children could remain living with the mother and be reintroduced to the father with therapeutic support.
The father’s proposal for a scaffolded reintroduction was lacking in essential detail. He suggested the appointment of a therapist for four to six weeks but identified no suitable practitioner. Both Dr Jones and the Family Report writer considered that only highly experienced clinicians could work effectively with this family. No such practitioners were identified in City B, and those suggested as possibly appropriate by the Family Report writer (W Centre, CAMHS, AA Hospital, and possibly Ms BB or Ms CC) are based in Melbourne. The children reside in City B; the father lives in Queensland.
There was no evidence regarding the cost of therapy, the parties’ capacity to pay, whether treatment could be delivered remotely, whether travel to Melbourne to attend a practitioner there would be required, when therapy could commence, its frequency, duration, or the likelihood of its success. The father also did not make clear whether he could even commit to regular travel to Victoria to participate in therapy. He said that he would do whatever he had to do for the children, but he also said he did not feel safe going to City B.
The father proposed that, after four to six weeks of therapy, the children would engage in supervised time with him twice weekly for several months, followed by a gradual increase to overnight time. None of this appears feasible while he continues to live in Queensland.
The father’s position at trial – namely, that he would not relocate to City B or within an hour’s drive even on a part time basis – was inconsistent with what he told Dr Jones in June 2024 and Dr Vergara in March 2025. I have already referred to the concerns raised by his treating psychiatrist in late 2024 and early 2025 about the impact on his mental health of travelling to City B in the context of attending a Magistrates’ Court hearing there.
The father himself expressed doubts about his own ‘alternate’ proposal and asserted that reunification efforts would be futile while the children lived with the mother.
Dr Jones also expressed significant concerns about the father’s entrenched negativity towards the mother and his ability to shield the children from his views, and indicated that substantial, intensive therapy for the father – over approximately six months – would be required to address these issues. The father did not indicate any willingness to undertake such therapy. No suitable practitioner was nominated.
The mother’s anxiety and her unshakeable belief that the father sexually abused the children present further, serious barriers. She has been strongly encouraged in her beliefs by C Mental Health Services and cannot envisage any safe reintroduction. Her ability to support the children in such a process is therefore highly doubtful.
The impact on the mother’s functioning and parenting capacity, and the likely flow on effect to the children is another important consideration.
Counsel for the father suggested that the children simply be observed spending time with him to gauge their reaction as a ‘first step’. That proposal was not put to the Family Report writer and contradicts the evidence of the experts that this is a complex situation and the children’s reintroduction to their father would require an experienced therapeutic professional. It also ignores the entrenched nature of the children’s narratives and the likelihood that they would feel unsafe and distressed.
I have no evidence as to how the children’s beliefs can be appropriately dismantled or what the next steps might be for the children to be reintroduced to their father. That includes what supports might be needed for each of the parents – who each presented with their own vulnerabilities – as they navigate that path.
In the absence of evidence as to how the children – and the parents – can be safely and practically supported through a process of reunification, no such order can be contemplated. Despite the Court raising this evidentiary gap during the hearing, the father made no application to reopen his case or provide further evidence.

Decision making

I am satisfied the parents are unable to work cooperatively to reach agreements about the children’s long-term welfare. The mother has, generally, made appropriate decisions for the children to date. She is an attuned and attentive parent. That includes attending to the children’s medical needs – having arranged appropriate assessments for them and attended medical appointments regarding concerns she has had as to their development and wellbeing.
There have been issues with school attendance from time to time with Y and X in particular experiencing some anxiety about attending. The mother said she works closely with the school to ensure the children are appropriately supported. That was corroborated by the school wellbeing officer in conversation with the Department of Families Fairness and Housing in May 2024.
In circumstances where I am satisfied the children should remain in her care, and there is no ability of the parties to consult each other, she shall also have sole decision-making responsibility for them.
The mother indicated she wanted to support the children to change their surnames from Prodi – being the surname they share with their father – should they wish to do so. She said X told her she does like to be known as X Prodi and has asked many times to change her name to X Frendo. She acknowledged that neither Y nor Z had suggested they wanted to change their surname.
I am not of the view that the mother should be at liberty to change the children’s surnames. There was no evidence from any expert as to the impact on the children if their names were changed – or not changed – at this stage. It is hardly unusual these days for children to have a different surname to their mother.
Pursuant to my orders, the children will not spend time or have communication with their father. I am concerned to sever their connection to him by name in the absence of evidence from any experts as to the impact that may have on them. Accordingly, I make no order in that regard.

Passports

The mother sought an order she be permitted to obtain passports for the children without the father’s permission, in the event she wanted to travel overseas with the children.
There were no submissions made in relation to this proposed order and accordingly I will not make it. I understand there is currently no overseas travel contemplated.

Injunction

Each parent sought a number of injunctions. The proposed injunctions were not the subject of submissions and I do not intend to make any such injunctions. Specifically, I will not injunct the mother from bringing the children into contact with her brother. In all the circumstances there seems little basis for such an order. At any rate, I accept the mother will act protectively without the necessity for an injunction.

CONCLUSION

I am very much aware that the result of the orders I am making is that the children will not see their father or communicate with him. He will also not be involved in making decisions regarding their care. This will be a loss for the children, denying them the opportunity of a relationship with him, and the opportunity to have a relationship with the broader paternal family. They will remain with their mother, who believes the children have been sexually abused by their father. They will likely continue to believe their father is not safe. Those considerations have very much been at the forefront of my mind.
However, after carefully taking into account all the evidence, including evaluating questions of safety, and considering the ability of each of the parents to appropriately care for the children, as well as weighing the various risks to the children’s psychological and emotional wellbeing on a range of potential outcomes, I am satisfied the orders I am making are in the children’s best interests. Regrettably this is a case in which the orders I am making appear to be the “least worst option”.
It may be that in the future, circumstances may change. To that end, I agree with the observations made by the Family Report writer that:
[i]n the longer term, as the children move into adolescence and develop greater abstract thinking and conceptual skills, can self–protect, have a clearer understanding of personal safety, have the benefit of maturation, greater independence and autonomy in the world, their capacity to have contact with [the father] may change from the current status where any contact is likely to be destabilising and potentially distressing. It may ultimately be in the children’s best interests if they are older, can navigate and conceptualise the complexities of their family situation, are afforded support to do so, and can have some agency to develop a way forward in this relationship along with all their family relationships.
However, I must determine the matter today, on the evidence that has been adduced before me.
Accordingly, for all the foregoing reasons, I make the orders as are set out.
